attributes.stem:
A 43-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for review of a pressure injury. She has C5 quadriplegia following a traumatic injury in childhood and lives at home with family support through the National Disability Insurance Scheme. Her parent reports she has lost five kilograms over the past three months. She has no other significant medical conditions and takes baclofen for muscle spasticity.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 3 cm x 4 cm area of partial thickness skin loss over her sacrum with exposed pink dermis. The wound bed is clean with no signs of infection. The surrounding skin is intact but erythematous.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a Grade 2 pressure injury, characterised by partial thickness skin loss with exposed dermis. The wound appears clean without signs of infection, making aggressive interventions unnecessary at this stage.

Foam dressing with regular repositioning is the most appropriate initial management. Foam dressings provide moisture balance and protect the wound while regular repositioning (every 2-4 hours) helps redistribute pressure and prevent further tissue damage. This approach addresses both wound healing and prevention of deterioration.

Surgical debridement would be excessive for a clean Grade 2 pressure injury. This intervention is typically reserved for deeper wounds with necrotic tissue or those that fail to respond to conservative management.

Negative pressure wound therapy is generally reserved for deeper (Grade 3 or 4) pressure injuries and is not necessary for this superficial wound.

Silver-containing dressings are antimicrobial dressings primarily indicated for infected wounds. As there are no signs of infection in this case, a silver dressing would be unnecessary.

Topical antibiotics are not indicated as there are no signs of infection. Their use in uninfected wounds can contribute to bacterial resistance and may cause local skin reactions.

Additional important aspects of management include nutritional assessment given the recent weight loss, review of pressure-relieving equipment, and education of carers about regular repositioning techniques. A multidisciplinary approach involving the patient's care team, dietitian, and wound care nurse would be beneficial.
learningPoints: Grade 2 pressure injuries involve partial thickness skin loss with exposed dermis and should be managed with appropriate dressing and pressure relief.
Regular repositioning and pressure redistribution are essential components of pressure injury prevention and management.
Nutritional assessment and optimisation are crucial in pressure injury management.
references: Avsar P, Moore Z, Patton D, O'Connor T, Budri AM, Nugent L. Repositioning for preventing pressure ulcers: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of wound care. 2020 Sep 2;29(9):496-508. Available online at ; https://www.magonlinelibrary.com/doi/full/10.12968/jowc.2020.29.9.496

Hsieh J, Benton B, Titus L, Gabison S, McIntyre A, Wolfe D, Teasell R. Skin integrity and pressure injuries following spinal cord injury. Spinal cord injury rehabilitation evidence. Vancouver, BC: Spinal Cord Injury Research Evidence (SCIRE) Professional Project. 2020:1-23. Available online at ; https://scireproject.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/skin-integrity-and-pressure-injuries_V7.pdf
